content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 7, 1991

R. M. Murakami
Assistant Commissioner
Department of Corporations
3700 Wilshire Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90010-3001

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-260

Dear Mr. Murakami:

You have requested advice on behalf of the Department of
Corporations concerning the new gift and honoraria limits of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since you have not named a
specific public official on whose behalf you have requested this
advice, we can only provide the following informal responses to
your questions.²

Moreover, please be aware that the Commission's jurisdiction
extends only to the Act. (Sections 81000-91015.) The Commission
has no jurisdiction to advise on or enforce other provisions of
the law which may apply to your situation.

QUESTION

Are scholarships which pay for tuition to the National School
for State Credit Union Examiners and necessary travel and
accommodations which are presented to credit union examiners at
the Department of Corporations gifts under the Political Reform
Act?

___________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-260
Page 2


CONCLUSION

Payment of tuition to the National School for State Credit
Union Examiners is informational material which is exempted from
the provisions in the Act concerning gifts. However, travel and
accommodations provided to examiners would be gifts, unless
provided by a bona fide public or private educational institution,
as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, or as
accepted by the Department of Corporations pursuant to In re
Stone.

FACTS

The National Association of State Credit Union Supervisors
("NASCUS") is a composed of public officials who regulate credit
unions throughout the country. In addition, credit unions may
join NASCUS as associate members.

NASCUS operates a nonprofit educational foundation, the
National Institute for State Credit Union Examination ("NISCUE"),
which was formed pursuant to Internal Revenue Code Section
501(c)(3). NISCUE periodically conducts the National School for
State Credit Union Examiners (the "program") which is a continuing
education program for credit union examiners. The program
provides lectures, discussion groups and case studies in order to
enhance the skills of credit union examiners.

The program is funded through tuition charges and fees paid
by states that use the program. However, NISCUE has also
implemented a scholarship system. Any person may contribute to
the scholarship fund, including credit unions. The donations are
presented directly to NISCUE who manages the distribution of the
scholarships.

Scholarships are presented to examiners throughout the
country to attend the program, including examiners of the
California Department of Corporations (the "department"). NISCUE
permits donations to be designated for specific examiners,
however, the Department of Corporations will only accept
nondesignated scholarships. The department internally selects the
examiner to attend the program. The scholarship covers tuition,
travel and accommodations. If the scholarship fails to cover all
the expenses associated with the program, the Department of
Corporations pays the remainder.

ANALYSIS

Source of the Gifts

Section 89505(a) provides, in pertinent part:

No member of a state board or commission, and
no designated employee of a state agency, shall
accept gifts with a total value of more than two

Our File No. I-91-260
Page 3


hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year
from any single source, if the member or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income
or gifts from that source on his or her statement
of economic interest.

You have asked whether payments made by NISCUE for travel,
accommodations and tuition, in the form of a scholarship to a
state credit union examiner in the Department of Corporations, is
a gift to the official.

Tuition

We have advised in that past that where a public official
receives free admission to an informational meeting or conference,
the payment for the admission is exempt from disclosure as
informational material. (Section 82028(b)(1); Kolodney Advice
Letter, No. A-87-182; Douglas Advice Letter, No. A-90-128, copies
enclosed.) Consequently, the portion of the NISCUE scholarship
attributable to the costs of training and materials associated
with the training are not subject to the Act's limit on receipt of
gifts, nor are they disclosable gifts.

Travel and Accommodations

Section 89505(b) states that where a public official receives
a gift of travel, Section 89506 controls whether the gift is
subject to the new gift limit. Section 89506 provides:

        (a)  Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

* * *

        (2)  The travel is provided by a
government, a governmental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authority, a bona
fide public or private educational institution,
as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue
and Taxation Code, or a nonprofit
charitable or religious organization which is
exempt from taxation under Section 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person
domiciled outside the United States which
substantially satisfies the requirements for
tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of
the Internal Revenue Code.

Section 89506(a)(2), emphasis added.

Our File No. I-91-260
Page 4


A training course in credit union examination is related to
the positions held by the examiners and related to a governmental
purpose. Further, it appears that NISCUE may be a bona fide
public or private educational institution as defined in Section
203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code. If this is the case, the
payments for travel and necessary accommodations would not be
subject to the gift limitations of the Act. However, the payments
would still be disclosable gifts (Section 87207) and would be
treated as economic interests for purposes of the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act. (Sections 87100, et seq.)

If NISCUE is not a bona fide public or private educational
institution, the provision of travel and accommodations to public
employees would be a gift for purposes of the gift limits,
conflict-of-interest and disclosure provisions of the Act.
Moreover, even if NISCUE is a bona fide public or private
educational institution, it appears from your facts that the true
source of the gifts may be the sponsors rather than the NISCUE.
(Bell Advice Letter, No. A-91-077, copy enclosed.) If this is the
case, the exception in Section 89506 would not apply.

The Stone Opinion

In 1977, the Commission determined that under some
circumstances a gift of travel and accommodations used by a public
official could be structured in such a way that the gift was in
fact made to the agency rather than the public official. (in re
Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52, copy enclosed.) For the Stone
exception to apply, all of the following must occur:

1. The donor must intend to donate the gift to the public
entity and not to the official;

2. The public entity must exercise substantial control over
use of the gift;

3. The gift must be generally available to agency personnel
in connection with agency business without regard to official
status. The donor cannot have limited the use of the gift to
specific persons or high level employees; and

4. The making and use of the gift must be formalized in a
written public record of the public entity which embodies the
standards set forth above.

The rationale behind the Stone opinion was that where travel
expenses and accommodations were of the type that the agency would
normally have paid for, the true benefit of the gift was afforded
to the agency not the official. Consistent with this exception,

Our File No. I-91-260
Page 5


where the Stone factors exist, the official has no disclosure
obligations with respect to the gift of travel or accommodations.
This is true regardless of the source of the gift.

You stated that the Department of Corporations handled the
distribution of the scholarship funds within the agency and that
where the scholarship did not cover all the costs associated with
the school, the department would pay the difference.
Consequently, where the Department of Corporations complies with
the Stone requirements with respect to the scholarships to the
department's examiners, the examiners will not have received a
gift for any purpose under the Act.

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

Enclosures
